result:
The rain doesn't fall here the way it does in other cities. In this place it arrives sideways, almost apologetic, like it knows it's interrupting something important. I've been watching it from my window for twenty-seven minutes now, counting the droplets that manage to cling to the glass before the wind bullies them away.

Sometimes I wonder if trees dream. Not in the poetic way people usually mean it, but literally—whether their root systems process the day's chemistry into something like narrative while we sleep. The massive oak outside my building has been here since 1893. It has outlived every human who planted it, every war, every economic collapse. Its leaves are currently turning that particular shade of exhausted green that signals summer is finally losing the argument.

I keep thinking about how we've managed to make loneliness into a product. There are entire industries built around making people feel just connected enough to stay disconnected. We scroll past each other in perfect silence while our thumbs develop early arthritis. Meanwhile the pigeons on my fire escape have more genuine community than most neighborhoods I know.

The rain has stopped. Just like that. The streetlights are reflecting off wet pavement in long golden streaks that remind me of Japanese woodblock prints. A woman in a red coat is walking her dog beneath my window, both of them moving with that particular post-rain lightness, as if the world has been reset.

I should probably close the window now. But I won't. Not yet. Some moments deserve to be allowed inside, even if they make the floor wet. 

(Word count: 248)
